did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses transfer learning-based Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including DenseNet201, Inception-v3, Inception-ResNet-v2, and Xception for multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images (Melanoma, BCC, AK, BKL, DF, VASC, SCC, NV). It employs an ensemble blending technique with a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) meta-learner to obtain classification results on the ISIC 2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a multiclass skin lesion classification system using transfer learning-based CNNs (DenseNet201, Inception-v3, Inception-ResNet-v2, Xception) and an ensemble blending technique. It describes specific methodology (dataset preparation, cropping strategy, model architecture modifications, training pipeline), reports quantitative results (balanced multi-class accuracy of 81.2% on the ISIC 2019 dataset), and is published in the proceedings of the 2021 Seventh International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII).

title: A Multiclass Skin Lesion classification approach using Transfer learning based convolutional Neural Network
journal: 2021 Seventh International Conference on Bio Signals, Images, and Instrumentation (ICBSII)
brief_summary: This study addresses the multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin lesions to aid in the early detection of skin cancer. The authors utilized transfer learning-based Convolutional Neural Networks, specifically DenseNet201, Inception-v3, Inception-ResNet-v2, and Xception, on the ISIC 2019 dataset. To handle uncropped images, a robust cropping strategy based on watershed segmentation was implemented. The predictions from these base classifiers were combined using an ensemble blending technique with a Multi-Layer Perceptron meta-learner. The proposed approach achieved a balanced multi-class accuracy of 81.2% on the compiled dataset. The results indicate that the ensemble model performs reasonably better than individual base classifiers for this classification task.

all_task_types.target: 8 discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesions, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Melanocytic Nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images. The model predicts one of 8 specific lesion classes using a softmax activation function. The primary metric reported is Balanced Multi-class Accuracy (BMA).

datasets_used.name: Compiled ISIC 2019 Dataset (Source: BCN20000, HAM10000, MSK)
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset of dermoscopic images provided by the ISIC organization, compiled from the BCN20000, HAM10000, and MSK datasets. It contains 25,331 images in JPEG format with inconsistent dimensions (600x450 to 1024x1024). The study used a subset of this data, splitting it into two training subsets (Dataset-1 and Dataset-2) and a separate test subset. Images were cropped using a watershed-based strategy and resized to 480 pixels.

use.training: Used to train four base classifiers (DenseNet201, Inception-v3, Inception-ResNet-v2, Xception) via transfer learning. The study created two subsets: Dataset-1 (4,999 images) and Dataset-2 (7,900 images) for training.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate held-out set; the paper mentions 'validation-split of dataset-1' in Figure 5 but does not provide specific counts or distinct usage separate from the training subsets in the text.
use.testing: A separate compiled test dataset containing 4,242 images was used to evaluate the final prediction performance of the ensemble model.
use.other: The original ISIC 2019 test dataset provided by the organization was not used; a custom test set was compiled.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the performance of individual base classifiers (DenseNet201, Inception-v3, Inception-ResNet-v2, Xception) and the ensemble model on validation and test datasets.
To provide secondary performance metrics for evaluating the classification results of base models and the ensemble model.
To handle class imbalance during the training of base models by assigning different weights to each class.
To prevent overfitting by monitoring validation loss and stopping training when it stops improving.

statistical_methods_used.method: Learning rate scheduling (reduction by factor of 10)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To adjust the learning rate during fine-tuning and when validation loss plateaus to aid convergence.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the ensemble model and base classifiers were made using descriptive statistics (Balanced Multi-class Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, F1 Score) without statistical significance testing.

Dataset-1.notes: The ensemble model achieved a Balanced Multi-class Accuracy (BMA) of 64.7% on the test dataset. The paper notes that the ensemble model performs reasonably better than the base models.

Dataset-2.description: Training subset with 7,900 images (more skewed class distribution than Dataset-1)
Dataset-2.notes: The ensemble model achieved a Balanced Multi-class Accuracy (BMA) of 81.2% on the test dataset. The paper states that performance increased with the larger dataset size (7,900 images) compared to Dataset-1.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of four pre-trained CNNs (DenseNet201, Inception-v3, Inception-ResNet-v2, Xception) combined via blending with a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) meta-learner
architecture_used.pretraining: All base models were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset
architecture_used.input_size: Base models expect inputs of 224x224 (DenseNet201) or 299x299 (Inception-v3, Inception-ResNet-v2, Xception); dataset images were resized to 480 pixels before processing

architecture_details.modifications: Original classifier parts replaced with: Batch Normalization layer -> Dropout layer -> Global Average Pooling layer -> Dense layer (512 neurons) -> Dropout layer -> Classification layer

training_details.learning_rate_schedule: Initial LR 1e-3 for first 3 epochs; fine-tuning LR 1e-4 starting from 4th epoch; LR reduced by factor of 10 if validation loss plateaus for 8 epochs until reaching 1e-6
training_details.epochs: 3 epochs for initial training (frozen layers); 50 epochs for fine-tuning (unfrozen layers)
regularization: Dropout layers in the modified head
Early stopping based on validation loss
training_details.augmentation: Online augmentation: vertical/horizontal flipping, rotation, zooming, shearing, width-shift

preprocessing_techniques: Cropping strategy for uncropped images using watershed segmentation: creation of elevation map using Sobel gradient, marker detection with low threshold (0.3), watershed transform to fill regions, connected component labeling to identify lesion region based on area and extent (>0.5), and bounding box calculation using centroid and major/minor axes.
Resizing images to a constant size of 480 pixels preserving aspect ratio.
Histogram corrections applied to some images in the HAM10000 subset.
Online data augmentation during training: vertical/horizontal flipping, rotation, zooming, shearing, and width-shift.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied on the fly during training. Techniques included vertical flipping, horizontal flipping, rotation, zooming, shearing, and width-shift. No specific probabilities or ranges were reported.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble approach using blending with a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) meta-learner outperformed individual base classifiers (DenseNet201, Inception-v3, Inception-ResNet-v2, and Xception) in multiclass skin lesion classification.

The ensemble model achieved a balanced multi-class accuracy (BMA) of 81.2% on the compiled test dataset derived from ISIC 2019, demonstrating the effectiveness of the transfer learning-based CNNs for this task.

The robust cropping strategy based on watershed segmentation effectively handled uncropped dermoscopic images, mitigating the negative impact of inconsistent image dimensions and background noise on classifier performance.

Increasing the training dataset size from 4,999 images (Dataset-1) to 7,900 images (Dataset-2) resulted in improved classification performance, with the ensemble model's BMA increasing from 64.7% to 81.2%.

The study confirms that deep learning-based classification systems can achieve performance levels comparable to or exceeding those of certified dermatologists, supporting their potential utility in early skin cancer detection.
The use of class-weighted cross-entropy loss was necessary to address the significant class imbalance present in the dermoscopic image datasets.

The authors acknowledge that the original ISIC 2019 test dataset was not used; instead, a custom compiled test set was utilized, which may limit direct comparability with other ISIC challenge submissions.
important_remarks: The study uses a custom compiled test dataset (4,242 images) rather than the official ISIC 2019 test set, which limits direct comparability with other ISIC challenge submissions.

The training datasets (Dataset-1 and Dataset-2) are subsets of the original ISIC 2019 data, selected to maintain class imbalance but potentially introducing selection bias compared to the full distribution.
Dataset-2, which yielded the best performance (81.2% BMA), has a more skewed class distribution than Dataset-1, raising concerns about generalizability to balanced clinical populations.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons between the ensemble model and base classifiers.

The ensemble model achieved a Balanced Multi-class Accuracy (BMA) of 81.2% on the test set, but precision (73%) and F1 score (56%) remain moderate, indicating potential issues with false positives or minority class detection.
High specificity values (97-98%) across models suggest a bias towards predicting negative cases or benign lesions, potentially at the expense of sensitivity for malignant classes like Melanoma.

The robust cropping strategy using watershed segmentation was necessary to handle uncropped images with inconsistent dimensions, but this preprocessing step may introduce artifacts or exclude valid lesion contexts.
The study acknowledges that the 'unknown' (UKN) class from the original dataset was excluded, limiting the model's applicability to real-world scenarios where undiagnosed lesions are common.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal compiled test set derived from the same source datasets (BCN20000, HAM10000, MSK).
The ensemble blending technique uses a non-linear Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) as a meta-learner, which may increase model complexity and deployment overhead compared to simpler averaging methods.
